title: Machaerium ferox
intro: Machaerium ferox, also known as the "wild ironplow" or "machaerium", is a plant species native to the tropical forests of Central and South America, belonging to the legume family (Fabaceae). The plant's roots and bark have been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various health conditions, including fever, rheumatism, and digestive issues, due to its potential anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of Machaerium ferox include flavonoids, saponins, and terpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In herbal medicine, Machaerium ferox is commonly prepared as a decoction or infusion, as well as in the form of tinctures or extracts, to harness its potential health benefits.
taxonomy: Machaerium ferox is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Fabaceae, commonly known as legumes. This plant is part of the genus Machaerium, which consists of over 100 species. Machaerium ferox is native to tropical regions, particularly in Central and South America. The plant is also known to be part of the subfamily Faboideae. As a member of the Fabaceae family, it has characteristic legume fruits and is often found in humid, tropical environments.
